preservation_note: All five compacted IDs (K01-K05) are heavily externally cited: K01 at narrative §7.2.1 + tests/test_prefix_cache_index.py:3 + tests/test_radix_tree.py:3; K02 at narrative §7.2.4 + tests/test_prefix_cache_index.py:4,40 + reviews/review-report.md:71; K03 at tests/test_block_hash.py:60,75,102,120 + tests/test_prefix_cache_manager.py:4,26,45 + test-report.json:29; K04 at narrative §7.5.2 + L757 mapping + tests/test_paged_integration.py:4,45 + reviews/review-report.md:75; K05 at narrative §7.4.4 (full subsection) + impl-notes.md:130 (cross-ch ref to Ch05 K05 — different module but co-cited) + tests/test_prefix_cache_manager.py:108 + reviews/review-report.md:73. To prevent dangling references, the in-file replacement is a SINGLE consolidated section labeled 'K01-K05 (compacted summary)' that retains all five `### KXX:` ID anchors so grep '## K01:' / '## K02:' / ... still resolves at the heading level (with the new heading being `### K01:` etc., still grep-friendly).

facts.title: Hash table, not radix tree — vLLM's prefix cache is a flat dict
facts.fact_full: vllm/v1/core/block_pool.py:L34-L127. The prefix cache is `cached_block_hash_to_block: dict[BlockHashWithGroupId, KVCacheBlock | dict[int, KVCacheBlock]]`. Per-block lookup is O(1); the chained-hash invariant gives the 'tree property' (miss at K → miss at K+1) without the tree. Microbench in Ch07 demo §2 shows hash table is ~4.6x faster than a hand-rolled RadixTree on realistic prompt lengths. Don't say 'vLLM uses a radix tree' — that's a common misconception, possibly from SGLang's RadixAttention literature.

facts.title: Append-only block_id assignment — no deduplication at insert
facts.fact_full: block_pool.py:L48-L52 NOTE: 'we don't check if there is already an identical block in the cache. This is because we want to make sure the allocated block IDs won't change so that block tables are append-only.' Why: PagedAttention reads block_table mid-decode; if dedup re-pointed a request's block_id, the GPU would silently corrupt output. Cost: BlockHashToBlockMap has a dict-of-blocks fallback branch (block_pool.py:L83-L86) for the rare collision case where two physical blocks share one hash key.

facts.title: Chained hash invariant — H_k depends on H_(k-1) transitively
facts.fact_full: kv_cache_utils.py:L539-L566 (hash_block_tokens). Each block hash includes the parent block's hash in its payload. Hence: if H_k matches across two requests, then `tokens[0..(k+1)*B]` must be byte-identical. This is THE property that lets `find_longest_cache_hit` (single_type_kv_cache_manager.py:L473-L483) `break` on the first miss — the comment there reads `'if a block hash is not in cached_block_hash_to_id, the following block hashes are not computed yet for sure'`. Tests must NOT assume any non-leading block can hit independently.

facts.title: max_cache_hit_length = num_tokens - 1 to preserve logits
facts.fact_full: kv_cache_manager.py:L208 has the magic line `max_cache_hit_length = request.num_tokens - 1`. Even if an entire prompt hits the cache, vLLM forces ONE token of recompute to produce logits for that position. Without this, a 100%-cache-hit request has no logits to sample → RuntimeError downstream. Small but load-bearing. Also has a comment about block-size alignment forcing recompute of an entire trailing block — `removing this limitation could slightly improve performance in the future`.

facts.title: Lazy eviction inside get_new_blocks, not a separate sweep
facts.fact_full: block_pool.py:L322-L352 (get_new_blocks). Eviction happens inline when popping the LRU head — `_maybe_evict_cached_block(block)` is called on each popped block (L341, L347). No background thread. Why: (a) no GIL contention; (b) eviction work scales with allocation work, not with cache size; (c) evict-on-pop guarantees cache index never points to a popped block. A 'cron-style sweep' would have to re-acquire the alloc lock — vLLM's design avoids that entire class of bugs.
